mnemonics.title: CAP Properties
mnemonics.content: REMEMBER: CONSISTENCY MEANS SAME DATA EVERYWHERE, AVAILABILITY MEANS ALWAYS RESPONDING, PARTITION MEANS KEEP WORKING WHEN CONNECTIONS BREAK

mnemonics.title: Choose Two Out of Three
mnemonics.content: DURING PARTITION, YOU MUST CHOOSE: CONSISTENCY OR AVAILABILITY - NOT BOTH. P IS INEVITABLE, SO PICK CA, CP, OR AP.

mnemonics.title: System Types
mnemonics.content: CP: CONSISTENCY FIRST (MongoDB, HBase) - AP: AVAILABILITY FIRST (Cassandra, DynamoDB) - CA: TRADITIONAL RDBMS (MySQL, PostgreSQL)

acronyms.usage: Fundamental theorem for distributed database design by Eric Brewer

acronyms.expansion: Partition, Availability, Consistency, Else, Latency, Consistency
acronyms.usage: Extension of CAP theorem that considers latency during normal operation (no partition)
Traditional databases like MySQL, PostgreSQL - typically CA systems
Non-relational databases that often prioritize availability and partition tolerance

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a triangle with C at one corner, A at another, and P at the third. Only TWO corners can be connected at a time - you must leave one out. During partition (P), you must choose between C or A.

visualTips.visualize: CP systems are like banks (accuracy matters, temporary unavailability OK). AP systems are like social media (always available, stale data OK).

Imagine nodes as cities connected by roads (network). When roads break (partition), you cannot deliver letters (data) to all cities. Either wait until roads fix (CP - inconsistent reads) or send letters to available cities anyway (AP - stale data).

analogies.analogy: Think of a bank ATM network. If connection between ATM and central server breaks, the ATM must either: refuse all transactions (CP - consistent, not available) or give out money from local cache (AP - available, potentially inconsistent).
Like Wikipedia page edits - when you edit, changes propagate to all servers over time. You might see slightly outdated content briefly, but eventually everyone sees the same version.
A single database server without replication is like CA - it provides consistent data and is always available, but if the server fails or network breaks, nothing works.
